nativeRange.habitat: Inshore reef environments at depths of around 10–20 m; has been observed in or near holes and crevices in reef structure

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting meaty foods including brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, and prepared frozen foods. Will also consume small ornamental crustaceans and invertebrates found in the aquarium.

goodWith: moderately aggressive fish larger than itself

summary: The Australian Multicolor Dottyback is a vividly colored reef species reaching around 10–13 cm, generally considered reef-safe but known to prey on ornamental shrimp. Best kept singly in tanks of 200 L or more with ample rockwork.
careNotes: The Australian Multicolor Dottyback (Ogilbyina novaehollandiae) is found along the Great Barrier Reef at depths of roughly 10–20 m, typically sheltering in holes and crevices in reef structure. In captivity it tends to be semi-aggressive and is generally advised to be kept singly — conspecific aggression toward other dottybacks and similarly shaped fish is well-documented. It is commonly considered reef-safe with respect to corals and most sessile invertebrates, but has been known to prey on ornamental shrimp and small crustaceans, making it a poor choice for mixed reef setups that include shrimp or small ornamental crustaceans. The species is reported to accept prepared and frozen foods readily and is generally considered suitable for beginner marine aquarists who can provide adequate rockwork and hiding spots. Males and females differ in coloration: males tend toward grayish tones with reddish heads, while females are described as ranging from pinkish to bright yellow or greenish. Large individuals of both sexes may darken to near-black. The species has been reared in captivity, suggesting aquaculture potential. A tightly-fitting lid is advisable, as dottybacks have been known to jump.
breedingNotes: No detailed captive-breeding information is currently available for this species.

fish.breedingNotes: The species has been reared in captivity, but detailed breeding protocols are not widely documented in the hobby literature.
